label: 70_resource_natural resource_curse_dutch disease
description: Countries rich in natural resources face difficulties in transforming subsoil wealth into productive assets. Despite potential benefits, abundant resources can exacerbate macroeconomic management issues and lead to the 'resource curse,' as seen with oil, gas, and mineral deposits in various nations.

earliest_articles.title: Oil windfalls and living standards: New evidence from Brazil
earliest_articles.excerpt: When a country or a community discovers oil, should they rejoice or mourn? Should citizens be thrilled or worried when their governments receive fiscal windfalls? It might seem that the answers to these questions are obvious. How could finding an abundance of natural resources or stumbling on greater resources for the…

earliest_articles.title: Oil and Democracy in Russia
earliest_articles.excerpt: Oil and Democracy in Russia Russia is often considered a perfect example of the so-called "resource curse"--the argument that natural resource wealth tends to undermine democracy. Given high oil prices, some observers see the country as virtually condemned to authoritarian government for the foreseeable future.…

earliest_articles.title: The Political Resource Curse
earliest_articles.excerpt: The Political Resource Curse The paper studies the effect of additional government revenues on political corruption and on the quality of politicians, both with theory and data. The theory is based on a version of the career concerns model of political agency with endogenous entry of political candidates. The evidence…

newest_articles.title: Easy money, easy spending: A new take on the resource curse
newest_articles.excerpt: When wealth or income suddenly increases, colloquially referred to as ‘easy money’, this may be directed towards unproductive use, referred to as ‘easy spending’. At the individual level, the psychology of easy money and its resulting effect on spending behaviour stem from a combination of our brain’s reward system…

newest_articles.title: The import channel of the resource curse
newest_articles.excerpt: Economies dependent on natural resources face macroeconomic and structural challenges known as the ‘resource curse’ (Hauk and Cabrales 2011, Ross 2012). One challenge traditionally emphasised in the resource curse literature is ‘Dutch disease’ wherein a natural resource discovery or price increase is accompanied by an…

newest_articles.title: Even a corrupt government may transfer resource revenues to citizens
newest_articles.excerpt: Resource-rich countries have weaker governance (Figure 1). This widely documented finding has led to the suggestion that the people in these countries may be better off if the government transferred the oil revenues directly to the citizens (see here, here, and here.) But this raises the question: Why would the elites…
